race.name: Ride Across Wisconsin
race.tagline: 175 miles of paved Wisconsin backroads in a single day. Rolling farmland, river valleys, and the Chippewa Valley — the Wisconsin Bike Fed's flagship fundraiser and the state's defining one-day cycling event. ~1,000 riders, 11 years of tradition, and rest stops stocked with Midwestern hospitality every 20 miles.

vitals.registration: Online via rideacrosswisconsin.com. Pay-per-mile pricing — $175 for the 175-mile option. Registration closes June 30. Special early-bird pay-per-mile week in early January.
vitals.feed_zones: Rest stops approximately every 20 miles, fully stocked with food, hydration, and mechanical support. Bike Patrol and certified mechanics on course. SAG support for riders who miss cutoff times.
vitals.cutoff_time: Rest stop cutoff times enforced — riders who miss cutoffs are sagged to the finish.

climate.primary: Mid-July Upper Midwest — warm, humid, with thunderstorm risk
climate.description: Mid-July in western Wisconsin delivers the full Midwestern summer experience. Daytime highs of 78-85F with moderate to high humidity, long daylight hours (sunrise before 5:30 AM, sunset after 8:45 PM), and generally sunny skies. However, the Upper Midwest summer is volatile — afternoon thunderstorms can build rapidly, and heat indices above 90F are common when humidity combines with summer temperatures. Morning starts are warm (60-68F) with dew on the roads. The Chippewa Valley's river corridors can channel both cooling breezes and headwinds. Wisconsin's July weather is rarely extreme but consistently humid, and 175 miles of sustained effort in 80F+ humidity is a different animal than 80F in dry Colorado air.
challenges: Humidity: Wisconsin summers are humid — sweat doesn't evaporate efficiently, increasing heat stress over 175 miles
Afternoon thunderstorms: rapid-building convective storms are common in July, with lightning risk on exposed roads
Heat index: 80F with high humidity can feel like 90F+, making hydration and electrolyte management critical
Exposed roads: rolling farmland offers minimal shade for much of the route
Temperature swing: morning starts in the 60s can climb to mid-80s by afternoon

terrain.primary: Paved country roads — flat to rolling
terrain.surface: Paved roads exclusively for the 2026 edition. The route loops through Eau Claire, Buffalo, Dunn, and Trempealeau counties on quiet backroads. Rolling farmland with river valley descents and climbs — not flat, but nothing approaching mountain terrain. Approximately 6,800 feet of climbing over 175 miles, concentrated in the rolling hills of North Trempealeau County. Road quality is generally excellent — Wisconsin county roads are well-maintained and relatively smooth. The route passes through small towns, along river corridors, and across agricultural landscapes.
features: 175-mile loop starting and finishing at Phoenix Park on the Eau Claire riverfront
Rolling terrain through the Chippewa Valley — not flat, but never steep
Approximately 6,800 ft of climbing spread across 175 miles of gentle rollers
Quiet paved backroads through Eau Claire, Buffalo, Dunn, and Trempealeau counties
River valley riding along the Chippewa and Buffalo rivers
Small-town passages through quintessential rural Wisconsin

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 51. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 50.

course_description.character: A 175-mile loop through western Wisconsin's Chippewa Valley, starting and finishing at Phoenix Park on the Eau Claire riverfront. The route heads south from Eau Claire into the rolling hills of North Trempealeau County before looping back through Eau Claire County. The terrain is classic driftless-area Wisconsin — not flat, not mountainous, but persistently rolling with short climbs and descents through river valleys, agricultural ridgelines, and forested corridors. Quiet county roads with minimal traffic. Small towns punctuate the route every 15-25 miles, each one a rest stop opportunity and a window into rural Wisconsin life. The river valleys of the Chippewa and Buffalo rivers provide scenic corridors of relief between the rolling farmland above. The character is pastoral, green, and honest — 175 miles of Wisconsin backroads that reward steady pacing and punish anyone who ignores the humidity.
course_description.signature_challenge: The cumulative toll of 175 miles in Wisconsin humidity. No single feature defines this course — no monster climb, no technical descent, no gravel sector. The challenge is relentless rolling terrain plus Midwestern humidity plus ultra-distance fatigue. The rollers that felt easy at mile 20 feel like walls at mile 140. The humidity that felt manageable at 8 AM feels oppressive at 2 PM. And the distance — 175 miles is 175 miles regardless of terrain. Your fueling plan, your hydration strategy, and your pacing discipline will determine whether you finish strong or barely finish at all.
suffering_zones: The Riverfront Rollout — Phoenix Park, Eau Claire
The First Rest Stop Stretch — Into Dunn County
The Final Push — Back to Eau Claire

logistics.airport: Chippewa Valley Regional Airport (EAU) in Eau Claire has limited commercial service — United Express connecting flights through Chicago O'Hare and seasonal Sun Country service. Most out-of-state riders should fly into Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport (MSP), which is 90 minutes west of Eau Claire and has direct flights from every major US city. Alternatively, fly into Madison (Dane County Regional, MSN, 3 hours southeast) or Milwaukee (General Mitchell, MKE, 4.5 hours southeast).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Book Eau Claire hotels early — the event recommends lodging partners and rooms fill quickly during RAW weekend. Hotels in downtown Eau Claire and along I-94 run $100-160/night. Campgrounds in the area are also an option. The loop format means you start and finish at Phoenix Park — no one-way logistics, no shuttle needed, your car stays where you left it. Arrive Friday for packet pickup, ride Saturday, recover Sunday. Eau Claire's downtown is walkable with restaurants, breweries, and the Eau Claire riverfront.
logistics.food: Rest stops every 20 miles provide food, snacks, and hydration — fueled by volunteers and local sponsors. Together Farms, Northwoods Brewpub, and Dells Mill are notable stops on the route. Eau Claire's downtown dining scene includes craft breweries (The Brewing Projekt, Lazy Monk), farm-to-table restaurants, and classic Midwestern fare. Pre-ride carb loading at a local restaurant the night before is part of the tradition.

history.origin_story: Ride Across Wisconsin was created by the Wisconsin Bike Fed — a 501(c)(3) nonprofit bicycle advocacy organization established in 1988 — as the organization's flagship fundraising event. The inaugural RAW in 2016 sent nearly 500 riders on a 175-mile one-day crossing from Dubuque, Iowa (across the Mississippi River) to Kenosha, Wisconsin on Lake Michigan. The concept was pure: ride across the entire state in a single day. For its first eight years, RAW maintained the cross-state point-to-point format, primarily using the La Crosse to Milwaukee corridor — a 235-mile route showcasing five state trails (La Crosse River, Elroy-Sparta, 400, Glacial Drumlin, and Hank Aaron). The event offered both mixed-surface and all-paved options. In 2024, the organizers revisited the La Crosse-to-Milwaukee route 'one last time' before pivoting to a new format. Starting in 2025, RAW shifted to a loop-based event centered on Eau Claire, offering 180-mile, 100-mile, 60-mile, and Family Ride options — the first time the event started and finished in the same location. The change was driven by a desire to showcase different parts of Wisconsin, give non-border communities the chance to host, provide more daylight for riders, and simplify logistics.
notable_moments: 2016: Inaugural RAW — nearly 500 riders cross Wisconsin from Dubuque to Kenosha, 175 miles in one day
2017: Route shifted to La Crosse to Milwaukee corridor, Team Trek-Segafredo pros rode with participants
2022: Route showcased Elroy-Sparta tunnels, Wisconsin Dells, Merrimac Ferry, and Trek World HQ
2023: New route options from La Crosse to Milwaukee with all-paved and mixed-surface choices
2024: Final La Crosse-to-Milwaukee edition — the last cross-state point-to-point RAW
2025: Format pivot to Eau Claire-based loop — 180-mile and 100-mile options, ~1,000 riders at Phoenix Park
history.reputation: Wisconsin's defining one-day cycling event and the Bike Fed's largest annual fundraiser. Well-regarded in the Upper Midwest cycling community as a challenging, well-supported, and mission-driven ride. Growing in recognition but still a regional event finding its identity after the 2025 format change.

biased_opinion.summary:
RAW is Wisconsin on two wheels. One hundred seventy-five miles of paved backroads through the Chippewa Valley — rolling farmland, river corridors, small towns with grain elevators and church steeples, and rest stops staffed by volunteers who genuinely want you to eat another cookie. The Wisconsin Bike Fed has been running this event for eleven years, and the experience is warm, well-organized, and distinctly Midwestern: no pretension, no flash, just good roads and good people. The 2025 pivot from a cross-state point-to-point to an Eau Claire-based loop simplifies logistics and gives the event a home base, though purists who loved the La Crosse-to-Milwaukee traverse may mourn the loss of the 'across' in Ride Across Wisconsin.

The challenge here is pure distance. 175 miles on paved roads in July humidity is a genuine endurance test — not because the terrain is hard (it isn't, really), but because mile 140 in 83-degree humidity with rollers is where your fueling plan either holds or collapses. The community carries you. The rest stops every 20 miles are lifelines. The cause — supporting bike infrastructure and safety education across Wisconsin — gives the miles meaning beyond personal achievement. RAW is not trying to be Unbound or RAGBRAI. It is trying to be the best one-day cycling event in Wisconsin, and it is succeeding.
strengths: Community: ~1,000 riders united by the Bike Fed mission — rest stops feel like block parties, not gas stations
Distance: 175 miles is a legitimate ultra-endurance day that tests fitness, fueling, and mental toughness
Support: rest stops every 20 miles, bike patrol, certified mechanics, SAG service — you will not be left alone out there
Wisconsin backroads: smooth pavement, quiet country roads, rolling scenery through the Chippewa Valley
weaknesses: Not a race: zero competition, no timing, no podium. Competitive cyclists will be bored.
Humidity: mid-July Wisconsin humidity turns moderate heat into oppressive heat over 175 miles
Remote start: Eau Claire requires driving for most riders — the regional airport has minimal service
Identity in transition: the shift from cross-state epic to Eau Claire loop is still finding its narrative
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The ride you do because 175 miles of Wisconsin backroads in July, with a thousand other riders and a cause worth supporting, is exactly the right way to spend a Saturday.

final_verdict.one_liner: 175 miles of Wisconsin backroads. ~1,000 riders. The Bike Fed's flagship fundraiser and the state's defining one-day cycling event.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a well-supported ultra-distance day on smooth Wisconsin backroads. Yes if supporting bike advocacy gives your miles purpose. Yes if you are a Wisconsin or Upper Midwest cyclist looking for the region's signature one-day ride. Skip if you need competition, technical terrain, mountain scenery, or a nationally prestigious event. Skip if July humidity is a dealbreaker.
alternatives: RAGBRAI — the granddaddy of Midwestern charity rides, 7 days across Iowa, 10,000+ riders
Seattle to Portland — 206 miles city-to-city, 6,000+ riders, 47 years of PNW tradition
Pan-Mass Challenge — 192 miles across Massachusetts, cancer fundraiser, 6,000+ riders
Horribly Hilly Hundreds — Wisconsin's other signature ride, brutal climbing in the driftless area

citations.label: Ride Across Wisconsin (RAW) — Wisconsin Bike Fed
1,000 Cyclists, One Day: 2025 Ride Across Wisconsin — Volume One
Wisconsin Bike Fed's 2024 RAW Revisits La Crosse to Milwaukee Route One Last Time — Urban Milwaukee
2025 Ride Across Wisconsin — 180 MI Route — RideWithGPS
Event Report: Ride Across Wisconsin (Aug 27, 2016) — Bread And Water

videos.title: Cycling the Grand Illinois Loop
videos.description: The Grand lllinois Loop is an 535 mile bicycle route in northern Illinois from the Chicagoland area out to the Mississippi River and back again. The route follows mostly bike path, either rail-trail or canal towpath. But some of the route is on paved roads shared by cars. Most of these roads are quiet back country roads with light traffic. I started in the Western Suburbs in the town of Carol Stream, right next to the Great Western Trail. From there I rode west to find the Fox River Trail and followed it north to McHenry. I then cycled west through northern Illinois farm country on my way through Rockford and Freeport and into the Illinois Driftless Area. This hilly section goes past the Apple River Canyon area to Galena in the northeast corner of Illinois. Galena is on the Mississippi River so I followed it south through Savanna where I jumped on the Great River Trail all the way to the Quad Cities. South and East of the Quad Cities I picked up the Hennepin Canal Towpath near Colona and followed it to the Illinois River. There was a short section of road riding through LaSalle-Peru to connect up with the I&M Canal Towpath. The I&M took me all the way back to Joliet and Chicago to complete the Loop. What a grand adventure right off the front porch of the Chicagoland area accessible to millions of local residents.

videos.title: Bicycling Trempealeau County (Sjuggerud Rd)
videos.description: Tuesday night ride with BCTC members on the rolling hills of Sjuggerud Road between Coral City & Pigeon Falls, WI. This road is very new and smooth black top which  makes it ideal for roller ski traininga as well.
